ci-capstone-storage-report-practice.title: Load rows and print a report
ci-capstone-storage-report-practice.prompt: Connect storage and reporting services so rows become objects and objects become a report.
ci-capstone-storage-report-practice.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

ci-capstone-storage-report-practice.starterCode:
from services.storage import load_tasks
from services.reports import summary_report

tasks = load_tasks("data/tasks.txt")
print(summary_report(tasks))

sc-capstone-storage-and-reports-design.title: Check the design choice
sc-capstone-storage-and-reports-design.prompt: Which choice best matches the lesson goal for capstone storage and reports?
sc-capstone-storage-and-reports-design.hint: Choose the option that keeps object responsibilities clear.

help.concept: Load persisted rows into task objects and render useful reports through services.
help.hint_1: Look for the answer that reduces duplication without hiding behavior.
help.hint_2: A good OOP answer usually keeps behavior near the object that owns the data.

options.a: Put every class in main.py so imports are never needed.
options.b: Give each class or service a focused responsibility and import it where needed.
options.c: Avoid methods so every object exposes all of its data directly.
options.d: Copy the same logic into each subclass to avoid shared code.

try-capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch0.title: Parse saved task rows into objects
try-capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch0.prompt: Complete storage parsing for bug and feature rows.
try-capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch0.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

try-capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch0.starterCode:
from services.storage import parse_task

for raw in ["bug|Fix login|high|open", "feature|Export CSV|reports|done"]:
    task = parse_task(raw)
    print(task.label())

try-capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch1.title: Create a capstone summary report
try-capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch1.prompt: Complete the report service so it lists each task and counts open tasks.
try-capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch1.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

try-capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch1.starterCode:
from models.bug_task import BugTask
from models.feature_task import FeatureTask
from services.reports import summary_report

tasks = [BugTask("Crash", "high"), FeatureTask("Export", "reports", "done")]
print(summary_report(tasks))

concept.title: Capstone Storage and Reports: the idea
concept.bodyMarkdown: **Capstone Storage and Reports** is not just syntax. In this course it is practiced inside small projects where one file owns one clear part of the program. Load persisted rows into task objects and render useful reports through services.

multifile-shape.title: Capstone Storage and Reports: the file shape
multifile-shape.bodyMarkdown: The file layout matters. Models hold object state and behavior, services coordinate groups of objects, tests verify behavior, and `main.py` stays thin enough to read.

graded-habit.title: Capstone Storage and Reports: what the grader checks
graded-habit.bodyMarkdown: The grader checks behavior from the running project. A strong answer keeps imports working, keeps all files present, and proves the object behavior through output or assertions.
